then it will compile to exe/deb file and when start the tauri app, the hono start too ?

Exactly! Here's how it works:

## **Deployment Flow**

**1. Build Process:**
```bash
cd apps/desktop
npm run tauri build
```

**Outputs:**
- Windows: `src-tauri/target/release/bundle/msi/EragearCopilot_1.0.0_x64_en-US.msi`
- Linux: `src-tauri/target/release/bundle/deb/eragear-copilot_1.0.0_amd64.deb`
- macOS: `src-tauri/target/release/bundle/macos/EragearCopilot.app`

**2. Runtime Behavior:**
```
User clicks EragearCopilot.exe
       ↓
Tauri app starts (GUI window appears)
       ↓
Tauri spawns Hono server as subprocess
       ↓
User configures server in GUI
       ↓
Click "Start" button → Hono server runs in background
       ↓
Web UI connects to embedded server
```

## **Technical Details**

**Tauri app lifecycle:**
```rust
// On app start
fn main() {
    tauri::Builder::default()
        .setup(|app| {
            // Auto-start server on launch
            let app_handle = app.handle();
            tauri::async_runtime::spawn(async move {
                let _ = invoke_server_start(app_handle).await;
            });
            Ok(())
        })
        .run();
}
```

**Process hierarchy:**
```
EragearCopilot.exe (Tauri app)
├── GUI (Rust + React frontend)
└── server.exe (Hono backend) ← subprocess
    └── opencode (AI agent) ← subprocess
```

**User experience:**
1. **Install**: `sudo dpkg -i eragear-copilot_1.0.0_amd64.deb`
2. **Launch**: Click desktop icon or run `EragearCopilot`
3. **Configure**: Set port, agent, security settings in GUI
4. **Use**: Web UI automatically connects to embedded server

**Benefits:**
✅ Single installation file  
✅ No command line needed  
✅ Automatic server management  
✅ Professional desktop app experience  
✅ Easy for non-technical users  

Perfect for distribution! 🚀

You mean this, right ?
---
title: "Node.js as a sidecar"
source: "https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/"
author:
  - "[[Runtime Authority]]"
published: 2026-01-07
created: 2026-01-17
description: "The cross-platform app building toolkit"
tags:
  - "clippings"
---
[Skip to content](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#_top)

In this guide we are going to package a Node.js application to a self contained binary to be used as a sidecar in a Tauri application without requiring the end user to have a Node.js installation. This example tutorial is applicable for desktop operating systems only.

We recommend reading the general [sidecar guide](https://v2.tauri.app/develop/sidecar/) first for a deeper understanding of how Tauri sidecars work.

- Package a Node.js application as a binary.
- Integrate this binary as a Tauri sidecar.
- For this we use the [pkg](https://github.com/yao-pkg/pkg) tool, but any other tool that can compile JavaScript or Typescript into a binary application will work.
- You can also embed the Node runtime itself into your Tauri application and ship bundled JavaScript as a resource, but this will ship the JavaScript content as readable-ish files and the runtime is usually larger than a `pkg` packaged application.

In this example we will create a Node.js application that reads input from the command line [process.argv](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/process.html#processargv) and writes output to stdout using [console.log](https://nodejs.org/api/console.html#consolelogdata-args).  
You can leverage alternative inter-process communication systems such as a localhost server, stdin/stdout or local sockets. Note that each has their own advantages, drawbacks and security concerns.

An existing Tauri application set up with the shell plugin, that compiles and runs for you locally.

1. Let’s create a new Node.js project to contain our sidecar implementation. Create a new directory **in your Tauri application root folder** (in this example we will call it `sidecar-app`) and run the `init` command of your preferred Node.js package manager inside the directory:
	- [npm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-894)
	- [yarn](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-895)
	- [pnpm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-896)
	```sh
	npm init
	```
	We will compile our Node.js application to a self container binary using [pkg](https://github.com/yao-pkg/pkg) among other options. Let’s install it as a development dependency into the new `sidecar-app`:
	- [npm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-897)
	- [yarn](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-898)
	- [pnpm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-899)
	```sh
	npm add @yao-pkg/pkg --save-dev
	```
2. Now we can start writing JavaScript code that will be executed by our Tauri application.
	In this example we will process a command from the command line argmuents and write output to stdout, which means our process will be short lived and only handle a single command at a time. If your application must be long lived, consider using alternative inter-process communication systems.
	Let’s create a `index.js` file in our `sidecar-app` directory and write a basic Node.js app:
	```js
	const command = process.argv[2];
	switch (command) {
	  case 'hello':
	    const message = process.argv[3];
	    console.log(\`Hello ${message}!\`);
	    break;
	  default:
	    console.error(\`unknown command ${command}\`);
	    process.exit(1);
	}
	```
3. To package our Node.js application into a self contained binary, create a script in `package.json`:
	```json
	{
	  "scripts": {
	    "build": "pkg index.ts --output my-sidecar"
	  }
	}
	```
	- [npm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-900)
	- [yarn](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-901)
	- [pnpm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-902)
	```sh
	npm run build
	```
	This will create the `sidecar-app/my-sidecar` binary on Linux and macOS, and a `sidecar-app/my-sidecar.exe` executable on Windows.
	For sidecar applications, we need to ensure that the binary is named in the correct pattern, for more information read [Embedding External Binaries](https://tauri.app/develop/sidecar/) To rename this file to the expected Tauri sidecar filename and also move to our Tauri project, we can use the following Node.js script as a starting example:
	```js
	import { execSync } from 'child_process';
	import fs from 'fs';
	const ext = process.platform === 'win32' ? '.exe' : '';
	const targetTriple = execSync('rustc --print host-tuple').toString().trim();
	if (!targetTriple) {
	  console.error('Failed to determine platform target triple');
	}
	// TODO: create \`src-tauri/binaries\` dir
	fs.renameSync(
	  \`my-sidecar${ext}\`,
	  \`../src-tauri/binaries/my-sidecar-${targetTriple}${ext}\`
	);
	```
	And run `node rename.js` from the `sidecar-app` directory.
	At this step the `/src-tauri/binaries` directory should contain the renamed sidecar binary.
4. After installing the [shell plugin](https://v2.tauri.app/plugin/shell/) make sure you configure the required capabilities.
	Note that we use `"args": true` but you can optionally provide an array `["hello"]`, [read more](https://v2.tauri.app/develop/sidecar/#passing-arguments).
	```json
	{
	  "permissions": [
	    "core:default",
	    "opener:default",
	    {
	      "identifier": "shell:allow-execute",
	      "allow": [
	        {
	          "args": true,
	          "name": "binaries/my-sidecar",
	          "sidecar": true
	        }
	      ]
	    }
	  ]
	}
	```
5. Now that we have our Node.js application ready, we can connect it to our Tauri application by configuring the [`bundle > externalBin`](https://v2.tauri.app/reference/config/#externalbin) array:
	```json
	{
	  "bundle": {
	    "externalBin": ["binaries/my-sidecar"]
	  }
	}
	```
	The Tauri CLI will handle the bundling of the sidecar binary as long as it exists as `src-tauri/binaries/my-sidecar-<target-triple>`.
6. We can run the sidecar binary either from Rust code or directly from JavaScript.
	- [JavaScript](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-883)
	- [Rust](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-884)
	Let’s execute the `hello` command in the Node.js sidecar directly:
	```js
	import { Command } from '@tauri-apps/plugin-shell';
	const message = 'Tauri';
	const command = Command.sidecar('binaries/my-sidecar', ['hello', message]);
	const output = await command.execute();
	// once everything is configured it should log "Hello Tauri" in the browser console.
	console.log(output.stdout)
	```
7. Lets test it
	- [npm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-903)
	- [yarn](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-904)
	- [pnpm](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-905)
	- [deno](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-906)
	- [bun](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-907)
	- [cargo](https://v2.tauri.app/learn/sidecar-nodejs/#tab-panel-908)
	```sh
	npm run tauri dev
	```
	Open the DevTools with F12 (or `Cmd+Option+I` on macOS) and you should see the output of the sidecar command.
	If you find any issues, please open an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri-docs).

---

© 2026 Tauri Contributors. CC-BY / MIT
then run tauri = tun desktop app and run hono server ?

**Exactly!** ✅ Here's the complete flow:

## **What Happens When User Runs Tauri App**

**1. User Action:**
```
Double-click "EragearCopilot.exe" (or .deb package)
```

**2. Process Chain:**
```
EragearCopilot.exe (Tauri desktop app)
    ↓
GUI window opens (React frontend)
    ↓
User clicks "Start Server" button
    ↓
Tauri spawns sidecar: eragear-server.exe
    ↓
Hono server runs in background
    ↓
Web UI connects to http://localhost:3000
```

**3. User Experience:**
- ✅ Desktop app opens with GUI
- ✅ Configure port, agent, security in settings
- ✅ Click "Start" → Hono server runs automatically
- ✅ Web chat interface works in embedded browser
- ✅ Everything in one window, no separate terminals

**4. Technical Details:**
```javascript
// In Tauri React app
const startServer = async () => {
  // This runs your compiled Hono binary
  const command = Command.sidecar('binaries/eragear-server', [
    '--port', config.port
  ]);
  
  // Server runs in background
  await command.execute();
  
  // Web UI connects automatically
  window.location.href = `http://localhost:${config.port}`;
};
```

**5. Final Product:**
- One installer file
- One desktop shortcut  
- GUI + Server + Web UI all integrated
- Perfect for non-technical users! 🎯

**Alternative:** Keep web UI separate, user accesses via browser while Tauri app manages server in system tray.

Which approach do you prefer?
Hãy cho tôi biết cấu trúc hiện tại của project này.

Cấu trúc hiện tại của project **Eragear Code Copilot** là một monorepo sử dụng **Turbo Repo** và **Bun**. Dưới đây là tóm tắt các thành phần chính:

### 📂 Cấu trúc Thư mục Chính

```text
eragear-code-copilot/
├── apps/               # Các ứng dụng chính
│   ├── server/         # Backend Hono (ACP Client)
│   ├── web/            # Frontend React (Vite + Tauri)
│   ├── native/         # Ứng dụng Mobile (Expo)
│   └── server-ui/      # Giao diện quản lý server
├── packages/           # Các thư viện dùng chung (Internal Packages)
│   ├── auth/           # Cấu hình Better-Auth
│   ├── config/         # Cấu hình Shared (TypeScript, ESLint, v.v.)
│   ├── db/             # Drizzle ORM & Schema
│   ├── env/            # Quản lý biến môi trường (T3 Env)
│   └── shared/         # Types, Event Schemas, Helpers
├── docs/               # Tài liệu chi tiết về ACP, tRPC, Kiến trúc
└── AGENTS.md           # Hướng dẫn dành cho AI Agents (như tôi)
```

### 🚀 Chi tiết các Apps

1.  **`apps/server` (Backend):**
    *   **Tech Stack:** Hono, Bun, tRPC, `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`.
    *   **Vai trò:** Là **ACP Client**. Nó quản lý các AI Agents (như OpenCode, Claude Code) qua Stdio, cung cấp quyền truy cập file hệ thống và terminal cho Agent.
    *   **Giao tiếp:** Nhận yêu cầu từ Web/Native qua tRPC/WebSocket và chuyển tiếp đến Agent.

2.  **`apps/web` (Frontend & Desktop):**
    *   **Tech Stack:** React, Tailwind CSS, Shadcn UI, Tauri.
    *   **Vai trò:** Giao diện chat chính. Đặc biệt, thư mục `src-tauri` cho thấy ứng dụng này đã sẵn sàng để đóng gói thành app Desktop.

3.  **`apps/native` (Mobile):**
    *   **Tech Stack:** Expo, React Native, HeroUI Native.
    *   **Vai trò:** Phiên bản di động, cho phép quản lý các session chat và tương tác với Agent từ xa.

### 📦 Các Packages dùng chung

*   **`packages/shared`**: Chứa các định dạng sự kiện (events.ts) và types dùng chung giữa Client và Server để đảm bảo tính nhất quán của dữ liệu.
*   **`packages/env`**: Chia nhỏ cấu hình môi trường cho từng nền tảng (`server.ts`, `web.ts`, `native.ts`).

Dựa trên cấu trúc này, việc tích hợp **Tauri Sidecar** cho `apps/server` như bạn đã đề cập là hoàn toàn khả thi và phù hợp với thiết lập hiện tại của `apps/web`.
Tạo file `Claude.md` từ `AGENTS.md`
